content.method: Structured stakeholder mapping and elite interviews with political parties, church-maintainer representatives, and unions to assess coalition stability under hypothetical reform scenarios; process-tracing comparison against the Polish 2015-2017 reversal sequence; potentially a Delphi panel of Hungarian education-policy experts.
content.question: Would Hungary's political coalitions (church-maintained schools, conservative and liberal constituencies, teacher unions) sustain support for a structural track-delay reform across a multi-year implementation cycle, or does the Polish reversal pattern predict comparable erosion regardless of measured benefits?
content.why_it_matters: This determines the practical viability of T2's structural reform and the durability of any gains from a T6 pilot; if reversal risk is high regardless of design quality, resources may be better spent on more incremental, harder-to-reverse levers (T3, T4) or on building pre-commitment mechanisms before attempting structural change.
